description:
I observed the first-winter Iceland Gull on Sixmile Reservoir of the Boulder Reservoir complex.  Lighting was very good during the first couple of minutes of observation (view downward toward the gull in cloudy conditions), and I got some particularly good views of the gull from behind with spread wings on an ice shelf, followed by good views of the gull in flight.  For the rest of the time, the gull was farther away in poorer light, including when I photographed it, but I could still evaluate structure and relatively patterns of dark and light.   Gulls were actively feeding with mergansers and goldeneyes, and I saw the Iceland Gull several times in flight circling around the east end of the lake and diving, and a few times standing on ice shelves near the lake edge.

Since the gulls were moving around, there were good opportunities for size comparisons.  The Iceland Gull was larger than the Ring-billed Gulls but noticeably smaller than the Herring Gulls.  The head was smallish and rounded.  Bill was thin and of medium length; it was hard to see details of bill color, but it looked at least mostly dark.  Overall, the gull was biscuit colored and remarkably uniform in color.  (The gull appears grayish in the photos in poor light, but the real color was more light beige.)  Folded primaries were the same pale color as the body.  Spread wings were uniform in color, and the secondaries were just as pale as the rest of the wing.  No tail band was apparent.  Although lighting was insufficient to evaluate the "frostiness" of the bird's appearance, lighting was good enough to see that the upperparts were patterned with darker speckles.  Most notably, when the gull was standing on ice shelves, I could see through a scope that the tertials were significantly speckled.

Hundreds of gulls were present, and they were vocal, but I couldn't attribute any individual sound to any individual gull.
photographer: Photographer: David Dowell, David Dowell, David Dowell, David Dowell
